coredata.dc:title: Near-infrared-II photothermal ultra-small carbon dots promoting anticancer efficiency by enhancing tumor penetration
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Han, Yu
Liu, Hongmei
Fan, Miao
Gao, Shutao
Fan, Dehui
Wang, Zhenguang
Chang, Jin
Zhang, Jinchao
Ge, Kun
coredata.dc:description: Because of the particular environment of the tumor microenvironment, improving the deep penetration of drugs in tumor sites is a critical problem to improve the therapeutic effect of the tumor. The ultra-small nanoparticles can achieve deep tumor tissue penetration without modification, which shows tremendous significance in tumor therapy. In this work, the ultra-small permeable carbon dots (PCD) have been developed with near-infrared-II (NIR-II) window photothermal irradiation and good biocompatibility. These PCD showed multi-color fluorescence under visible light and photoacoustic signals under an excitation of 808 nm, guiding fluorescence and photoacoustic imaging for location and distribution in vitro and vivo. The PCD could penetrate the deep tissue in tumor spheroids and tissues. Meanwhile, the irradiated depth of the NIR-II window can provide sufficient photothermal energy with the deep penetration of PCD in tumor tissue to cause tumor ablation. Therefore, this PCD can be used as a safe, fluorescent, and photoacoustic imaging agent for guided NIR-II photothermal tumor therapy, which provides a new direction for the use of ultra-small carbon dots in anticancer therapy in the future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Ultra-small size
Tumor penetration
NIR-II photothermal
Tumor therapy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence and photoacoustic imaging of PCD

National Natural Science Foundations of China

Ministry of Education of the People's Republic of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundations of China (No. 31971304, 21807023), Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province (No. B2021202038, B2019201449), the Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei Basic Research Cooperation Project (No. 19JCZDJC64100), the Priority Strategy Project of the Key Laboratory of Medicinal Chemistry and Molecular Diagnosis of the Ministry of Education (ts2020003), the Hebei Province “Three Three Three Talents Program” (A202003001).and 2021 Hebei Province Doctoral Candidate Innovation Funding Project (CXZZBS2021011). In addition, the authors would like to thank Conghua Qi from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the TEM photograph and XPS analysis.

This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundations of China (No. 31971304, 21807023 ), Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province (No. B2021202038, B2019201449 ), the Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei Basic Research Cooperation Project (No. 19JCZDJC64100 ), the Priority Strategy Project of the Key Laboratory of Medicinal Chemistry and Molecular Diagnosis of the Ministry of Education ( ts2020003 ), the Hebei Province “Three Three Three Talents Program” ( A202003001 ).and 2021 Hebei Province Doctoral Candidate Innovation Funding Project ( CXZZBS2021011 ). In addition, the authors would like to thank Conghua Qi from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the TEM photograph and XPS analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The Characterization of the PCD. (A) schematic illustration of the route of synthesis. (B) Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image. (C) Atomic force microscope (AFM) image and other statistics. (D) HRTEM image. (E) Elemental mapping of C, N, O, and S. (F) XRD patterns. (G) Raman spectrum. (H) The full scan XPS spectrum. High-resolution C1s (I), O1s (J), N1s (K), and S2p (L) XPS spectra. (M) FTIR spectroscopy.

Fluorescence and photoacoustic characterization of the PCD. (A) UV–vis absorption from 400 to 900 nm. (B) Emissions spectra of the PCD (at various wavelengths in dilute aqueous solution). (C) Corresponding CIE diagram of the PCD. (D) Emissions pathways are illustrated in the simplified Jablonski diagrams, where the arrow colors represent the shift in the emissions wavelengths from blue to red (high to low energies). (E) Photoacoustic images of the PCD in a dilute, aqueous solution of different concentrations.

Fluorescence and photoacoustic imaging of the PCD. (A) Bright-field and fluorescence images of MCF-7 cells co-incubated with PCD under confocal 405, 488, and 561 nm excitation lights. (B) Fluorescence and photoacoustic signals in vivo (the fluorescence and photoacoustic imaging of the PCD in vivo at different time points after injection with PCD in situ). (C) The PCD in tumor-bearing mice at different time points after tail vein injection.

The photothermal ability of PCD under 1064 nm laser irradiation. (A) UV–vis absorption from 850 to 1200 nm. (B) Temperature changes of PCD solutions at different concentrations under irradiation (1.0 W/cm2). (C) Photothermal images of the PCD solutions (200 μg/mL) were captured at various times (1.0 W/cm2). Photothermal conversion ability (D) and the temperature variation over five on/off cycles (E) of 200 µg/mL PCD solution under irradiation (1.0 W/cm2). (F) Infrared thermal images of cells and tumors under 1064 nm laser irradiation (1.0 W/cm2). MCF-7 cells were incubated with different concentrations of PCD and MCF-7 tumor-bearing mice at eight hours post-injection with PCD for irradiating various times. The cell viability of MCF-7 cells without irradiation (G) and with irradiation (1.0 W/cm2, 5 min) (H) when treated with different concentrations of PCD for 24 h. ***p < 0.001 for different PCD groups vs. 0 μg/mL concentration group.

The deep permeation of PCD. (A) Schematic diagram of cell on 3D scaffolds treated with PCD under 1064 and 808 nm irradiation. (B) Cell viability when testing on 3D cell scaffolds treated with PCD under 1064 and 808 nm irradiation (1.0 W/cm2, 5 min). PCD permeation in tumor spheres (C) and solid tumors (D) at different time points. ***p < 0.001 for different concentrations of PCD vs. 0 μg/mL concentration group under 1064 nm irradiation; ###p < 0.001 for different concentrations of PCD vs. 0 μg/mL concentration group under 808 nm irradiation; &p < 0.05, &&p < 0.01 and &&&p < 0.001 for 1064 nm irradiation group vs. 1064 nm irradiation group at same concentrations of PCD.

Anticancer efficiency in vivo. (A) Timeline of the treatment schedule. (B) Mouse body weights. (C) Tumor volumes during the treatment. (D) Photographs of tumors excised from the mice. (E) H&E-stained and TUNEL images of tumors from the mice at the end of the treatment. ***p < 0.001 for PCD + NIR-II vs. PBS, PBS + NIR-II, and PCD groups, individually; ###p < 0.01 for PCD + NIR-II + Chicken vs. PBS, PBS + NIR-II, and PCD groups, individually.

head.ce:title: Near-infrared-II photothermal ultra-small carbon dots promoting anticancer efficiency by enhancing tumor penetration

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Medicinal Chemistry and Molecular Diagnosis of the Ministry of Education, Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology of Hebei Province, College of Chemistry and Environmental Science, Hebei University, Baoding 071002, PR China

Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology of Hebei Province
College of Chemistry and Environmental Science

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, Hebei Agricultural University, Baoding 071001, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life Sciences, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300072, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Affiliated Hospital of Hebei University, Baoding 071002, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Affiliated Hospital of Hebei University, Baoding 071002, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ultra-small carbon dots can promote anticancer efficiency by enhancing tumor penetration in breast cancer under near-infrared-II irradiation.

Because of the particular environment of the tumor microenvironment, improving the deep penetration of drugs in tumor sites is a critical problem to improve the therapeutic effect of the tumor. The ultra-small nanoparticles can achieve deep tumor tissue penetration without modification, which shows tremendous significance in tumor therapy. In this work, the ultra-small permeable carbon dots (PCD) have been developed with near-infrared-II (NIR-II) window photothermal irradiation and good biocompatibility. These PCD showed multi-color fluorescence under visible light and photoacoustic signals under an excitation of 808 nm, guiding fluorescence and photoacoustic imaging for location and distribution in vitro and vivo. The PCD could penetrate the deep tissue in tumor spheroids and tissues. Meanwhile, the irradiated depth of the NIR-II window can provide sufficient photothermal energy with the deep penetration of PCD in tumor tissue to cause tumor ablation. Therefore, this PCD can be used as a safe, fluorescent, and photoacoustic imaging agent for guided NIR-II photothermal tumor therapy, which provides a new direction for the use of ultra-small carbon dots in anticancer therapy in the future.

ce:section.ce:para: Because of the particular microenvironment of the tumor, such as abnormal vasculature, density extracellular matrix, and elevated interstitial pressure, it is difficult for drugs to spread or penetrate the tumor site.[1] Therefore the low drug concentration in the tumor tissue cannot eradicate all the tumor cells. The survived cancer cells will cause tumor regeneration and recurrence, forming a vicious circle, and even induce drug resistance of tumor cells.[2] Therefore, improving the deep penetration of drugs in tumor sites is a critical problem to be solved to improve the therapeutic effect of the tumor.

After accumulating at the tumor site, drugs need to penetrate the tumor tissue through a series of gaps and enter the tumor cells to exert their efficacy. During the penetration process, the size, shape, charge, and targeting modification of the nanoparticles will affect the diffusion rate of the nanoparticle at the tumor site, thus affecting its penetration ability. Some studies have demonstrated that ultra-small nanoparticles can achieve deep tumor tissue permeability without modification.[2,3] Huang et al. reported that ultra-small gold nanoparticles (2 and 6 nm) showed deep tumor penetration in vitro and in vivo.[4] Meanwhile, ultra-small Ag2S quantum dots can penetrate an entire tumor with a diameter of about 9 mm and effectively kill the tumor cells.[5] Likewise, ultra-small, iron-doped titanium oxide nanodots have been synthesized by a thermal decomposition strategy and can penetrate the deep tissue to kill tumors.[5,6] Therefore, the development of ultra-small nanoparticles with a particle size of<10 nm is of great significance for deep tissue permeability.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a kind of ultra-small nanoparticles with a diameter<10 nm, which own the fluorescence monitoring characteristics, good photothermal conversion, biological safety, and stability.[7] Based on increasing tumor penetration, the anticancer efficiency of CDs can be significantly improved by photothermal therapy.[8] Nevertheless, there are few works about the tumor penetration of ultra-small CDs. In this paper, permeable carbon dots (PCD) with a diameter of 5 nm were prepared by a one-step method. Moreover, PCD offered good fluorescence under visible light, photoacoustic imaging under an excitation of 808 nm, and photothermal therapy (PTT) effects at 1064 nm irradiation. Near-infrared-II (NIR-II) PTT irradiated by a laser in the 1000–1700 nm range has good prospects for application in tumor photothermal therapy because of its lower intensity, deeper tissue-penetration depth, and weak light scattering compared with near-infrared-I (NIR-I) light (750–900 nm).[9] Accompanied by the deep tumor tissues penetration, NIR-II dependent PTT efficiency could be enhanced with the permeability of PCD in tumor tissue. The tissue penetration depth of PCD was evaluated in tumor spheroids and tissues. Due to its photothermal properties and ultra-small particle size, ultra-small PCD can be an effective material for tumor therapy using deep tissue penetration and NIR-II dependent PTT.

One gram of urea and two grams of citric acid monohydrate were mixed in 10 mL of DMSO solution and packed in a 25-mL reaction kettle. Then, the reaction was run in an air oven at 160 °C for about six hours. Next, PCD was obtained via the density gradient centrifugation method when the reaction kettle's temperature had fallen to room temperature.[10]

The primary reagents, cells, animal models were listed in the Supporting Information. In addition, the structural and photoacoustic characterizations of PCD were also detailed in the Supporting Information. All the animal experiments were carried out at the Comprehensive Medical Experimental Center of Hebei University according to the Animal Welfare and Ethical Committee of Hebei University (Approval No. IACUC-2018025).

The fluorescence effect of PCD was measured by a fluorescence spectrometer (F-7000, Edinburgh Instruments, UK). When the excitation light of PCD was set from 360 to 540 nm at the concentration of 200 μg/mL, the emissions spectrum was recorded.

Intracellular imaging of PCD was captured by a laser scanning confocal microscope (LSM880, ZEISS, Germany) at 408, 488, and 561 nm after the human breast cancer cells, MCF-7, had incubated with PCD at 50 μg/mL for four hours.

Mouse imaging of PCD in vivo was conducted by a small animal fluorescence imaging system (IVIS Spectrum, Perkin Elmer, USA). MCF-7 tumor-bearing mice were injected in situ with 100 μL 2 mg/mL PCD at the tumors, and fluorescence in vivo was observed at 0, 2, 4, 8, and 24 h.

The photothermal effect of PCD was observed firstly at different concentrations via irradiation by a semiconductor laser unit (MIL-H-1064 and MIL-H-808, Changchun Laser Optoelectronics Technology, China) at 1064 or 808 nm for ten minutes. The temperature was recorded by thermal imaging equipment (Tix580, Fluke, USA).[11] The photothermal conversion efficiency of PCD was conducted at 1064 or 808 nm by the same method at a concentration of 200 μg/mL, and the measurements and calculation methods were detailed in the supporting information.[12] Furthermore, different concentrations of PCD were added into cultured MCF-7 cells in a 96-well plate and performed photothermal irradiation at a power of 1 W/cm2. Photothermal treatment was conducted for five minutes in the tumor-bearing mice after injection of PCD for eight hours. Meanwhile, thermal effects of the cells and tumor in vivo were captured by a Tix580 thermal camera.

The deep penetration of PCD was first evaluated on a cell-cultured scaffold to mimic tumor tissue. About two × 104 MCF-7 were seeded on a 3D cell scaffold with a diameter and height of 1 cm. Then, the scaffolds with the cells were placed into a 24-well plate and cultured for 24 h. Later, PCD was added with the concentrations of 0, 10, 20, 50, 100, 200, 300, and 500 μg/mL and further incubated for eight hours. Then, each well was irradiated by a NIR laser at 1064 or 808 nm for five minutes. A total of 24 h later, cytotoxicity was assessed by an MTT assay.

Next, the penetration effect of PCD was observed on the tumorspheres according to the fluorescence of PCD in different thicknesses of tumorspheres.[13,14] Briefly, about two hundred MCF-7 cells were inoculated in the 96-well plates with low viscosity, forming tumorspheres. When the volume of the cultured tumorspheres grew to 200–300 μm, they were transferred into the glass-bottle cell culture plates and added with PCD at a 200 μg/mL concentration for co-culturing 1, 2, and 8 h. Then, the tumorspheres were washed with phosphate-buffered solution (PBS), and images were captured via z-stack by a laser scanning confocal microscope.

The penetration effect of PCD was further evaluated in vivo by detecting the fluorescence of PCD in different regions of tumor tissues. In brief, paracancerous injection of 2 mg/mL of PCD into MCF-7 tumor-bearing mice for several hours, tumor tissues were removed and prepared for frozen sections. Then, near, medium, and far regions from the injection site of each tumor tissue were cut to get different sections. The fluorescence of PCD in the tissue sections was observed with a laser scanning confocal microscope.

The distribution of PCD was detected in the excised organs by the fluorescence of PCD. After being injected with 100 μL 2 mg/mL PCD by tail vein for different hours, MCF-7 tumor-bearing mice were executed, and their main organs were isolated and washed with PBS. Then, the fluorescence values of the organs at different time points were recorded by the small animal fluorescence imaging system.[15]

PCD with concentrations of 0, 10, 20, 50, 100, 200, 300 and 500 μg/mL were incubated with MCF-7 cells at 2 × 103 cells per well in a 96-well plate for 24 h. Each well was irradiated with a NIR laser at 1064 or 808 nm (1 W/cm2) for five minutes. Twenty-four hours later, cytotoxicity was detected by MTT assay and live/dead cell-staining with calcein and PI.[16]

Tumor-bearing mice were established by subcutaneous injection of about five × 105 MCF-7 cells, and the mice were randomly divided into six groups (five mice per group): (1) a PBS group, (2) a PBS + NIR-II group, (3) a PCD group, (4) a PCD + NIR-II group, (5) a PCD + NIR-II + Chicken group and (6) a normal mice group (referred to as Normal). Groups (1) and (2) received a 100 μL PBS trans caudal vein injection every four days. Groups (3), (4), and (5) received 100 μL of 2 mg/mL PCD injected via the caudal vein every four days. Groups (2), (4), and (5) underwent photothermal treatment for five minutes eight hours after injection. Moreover, group (5) had its tumor area covered with a millimeter-thick chicken breast. After treatment, the mice’s weight and tumor volume were detected every two days. Then, after 25 days of treatment, all nude mice were killed, and their hearts, livers, spleens, lungs, kidneys, and tumors were isolated and fixed. The histological morphologies of the above organs were observed via histological section and hematoxylin-eosin staining (H&E). The apoptosis of tumor tissues was measured by TdT-mediated dUTP Nick-End Labeling (TUNEL).[17] Tumor volume = Width × Width × Length/2.

All the data from cells were collected in three independent experiments, and the data from mice were collected in five mice. The data were presented as mean with standard error of mean by GraphPad Prism software. A two-tail Student's t-test determined the comparison in two groups. One-way ANOVA determined the comparison in the three or above groups. Two-way ANOVA determined the comparison in the tumor volume changes in different groups.

PCD was synthesized through a solvothermal method using citric acid and urea as raw materials and using DMSO as the solvent (Fig. 1
A). The PCD were well-dispersed in solution, quasi-spherical in shape, with an average diameter of 5 nm (Fig. 1 B) and heights of 1–3 nm (Fig. 1 C), respectively. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images showed that PCD possessed a high crystallinity like graphene with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm (Fig. 1 D). Elemental mapping results suggested that PCD were composed of C, N, O, and S elements (percentage composition of 82.16%, 8.70%, 8.80%, and 0.34%, respectively), which were uniformly distributed throughout the dots ( Fig. S1 in Supporting Information and Fig. 1 E). Moreover, X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns exhibited a sharp peak at 26.4° (Fig. 1 F), indicating that PCD had the typical (002) diffractions of graphitic carbon.[18-20] Raman spectrum of PCD showed that two pronounced characteristic sp3-hybridized and sp2-hybridized bands were located at 1350 cm−1 (the D band) and 1580 cm−1 (the G band), respectively. The intensity ratio of the two bands was 0.85 (Fig. 1 G), which also indicated the crystallization of PCD.[8,21-23] The chemical compositions of PCD were investigated through X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and a full spectrum scan further confirmed that PCD was composed of the elements C, N, S, and O (Fig. 1 H). For the carbonyl-containing species, the peaks at 284.4, 286.3, and 288.9 eV were attributed to CC or CC, CO, and C–OH groups, respectively (Fig. 1 I).[24] For the oxygen-containing species, the peaks at 531.7 and 533.1 eV were fitted O1s assigning to CO and C-OH/COC bonds (Fig. 1 J).[24,25] For the nitrogen-containing species, the two peaks at 399.4 and 401.3 eV were attributed to CNC and CNH (Fig. 1 K).[24,25] For the sulfur-containing species, two peaks at 163.4 and 164.6 eV corresponded to the CSC covalent bonds at S2p2/3 and S2p1/3 positions (Fig. 1 L).[19] The Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was further demonstrated that PCD had the stretching vibrations of the NH, OH, CO, and CC bonds at 3430, 3202, 1651, and 1398 cm−1, respectively (Fig. 1 M).[26] These peaks indicated the presence of nitrogen/carbonyl- and sulfur/carbonyl-containing groups in PCD, which demonstrated the N and S co-doped property of the PCD. Moreover, the zeta potential of PCD in an aqueous solution was −15.81 mV ( Fig. S2 in Supporting Information), which indicated that PCD had good stability and abundant negative charge on its surface, such as carboxyl.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence and photoacoustic imaging of PCD
ce:section.ce:para:
Fluorescence is the main character of carbon dots. The absorption peak of PCD solution in the ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectrum was from 400 to 900 nm, which was extended from the UV to NIR region (Fig. 2
A). When doped with different elements, CDs can exhibit different emissions. For example, the fluorescence emission spectra of CDs showed blueshift when doping of N, and the fluorescence emission spectra of CDs showed redshift when doping of S.[19,27,28] Elementary composition showed that PCD were co-doped with N and S ( Fig. 1 E), suggesting that fluorescence characterizes of PCD showed excitation-dependent emissions properties, the emission wavelength was excitation independent when the excitation wavelength was lower than 375 nm, but exhibited a redshift from 450 to 650 nm when the excitation wavelength was increased gradually from 400 to 540 nm (Fig. 2 B). The photoluminescence quantum yield of PCD was 0.816%. The Commission Internationale de L’Eclairage (CIE) coordinates of the fluorescence spectra recorded under different excitation wavelengths were presented in Fig. 2 C, which indicated that the emission color could be modulated from blue to deep red through a simple changing of the excitation energy. Emission pathways were also illustrated in a simplified Jablonski diagram that showed the changes of emission color and intensities corresponding to changes in the excitation wavelength (Fig. 2 D). The color and thickness of the arrow stand for the emissions color and intensity under different excitation wavelengths. The spectral width was relatively broad, and tiny tails can be recorded after extending to longer wavelengths. These excitation-dependent emissions features can be divided into three regions. In excitation wavelengths from 360 to 400 nm, the peak at 450 nm was the dominant component for the emissions spectra, and the band located at 520 nm gradually increases with the excitation wavelength. As the excitation wavelength increased from 400 to 500 nm, the emission peak redshifted obviously from 545 to 600 nm. In the excitation wavelengths between 500 and 540 nm, the emissions peak was mainly at 650 nm, with slight fluctuations in intensity. These phenomena suggested that the fluorescence of PCD was related not only to their carbon core but also to various functional groups and surface traps. To highlight the performance of PCD, we compared carbon dots synthesized in methanol, water, and ethanol under the same conditions. In aqueous solution, their colors were yellow, blue, light yellow, and purplish red, respectively, while the color of the PCD was black ( Fig. S3 in Supporting Information). Testing different concentrations of PCD prostheses in vitro found that PCD had obvious 808 nm photoacoustic signals (Fig. 2 E), which provides the possibility of photoacoustic imaging in-vivo.[24]

The fluorescence imaging ability of PCD was first performed by a confocal microscope in cells under different excitation lights. After incubating PCD for four hours, MCF-7 cells were irradiated with 405, 488, and 561 nm excitation lights, respectively, showing noticeable blue, green, and red fluorescence (Fig. 3
A). In addition, after the injection of PCD into mouse tumors in situ, bright fluorescence, and photoacoustic signals can be detected in vivo from 2 to 24 h (Fig. 3 B). As time went by, the PCD would be gradually metabolized in the body, but the fluorescence of the tumors could also be detected after 24 h, indicating that the fluorescence and photoacoustic signals of PCD were relatively stable in vivo. The distribution of PCD in vivo by tail vein injection in MCF-7 tumor-bearing mice was further identified in ex vivo organs by a small animal fluorescence imaging system. These mice were sacrificed at different time points after PCD injection. After two hours, PCD was mainly located in liver and lung tissues, and a small amount of PCD appeared in the tumor tissue (Fig. 3 C). As time went by, PCD in the tumor tissue increased gradually, and the fluorescence intensity of PCD was highest at eight hours after injection. However, PCD in the liver reached its maximum at four hours and decreased gradually, and finally, only a small amount of fluorescence was visible at 24 h. PCD in the lung decreased gradually after two hours, and no accumulation occurred after eight hours. A small amount of PCD was detected in the spleen at four hours, and no PCD was found at any other time point. Also, no evident PCD fluorescence was seen in the heart and kidney. It was concluded that PCD could accumulate in tumor tissue through the veins and would be gradually excreted from the body through liver and kidney metabolism.

Because of the deep depth of NIR-II irradiation, the prepared PCD showed the apparent redshift in absorption covered from 800 to 1200 nm (Fig. 3 A and Fig. 4
A) as well as an N-doping graphitic structure (Fig. 1 E and Fig. S1 in Supporting Information), which indicates its potential photothermal performance both in NIR-II and NIR-I region. Irradiated by a laser at 1064 or 808 nm, the photothermal conversion ability of the aqueous PCD solution was significantly enhanced with prolonged durations of irradiation and increased concentrations of PCD (Fig. 4 B-C and S4A-B in Supporting Information). Moreover, the photothermal conversion ability of PCD at 1064 and 808 nm was 77.25 and 83.72%, respectively (Fig. 4 D,
Fig. S4
C,
Fig. S5
A, and
Fig. S5
B in Supporting Information). After irradiation with a 1064 nm laser (1.0 W/cm−2) for ten minutes, the temperature of 200 μg/mL PCD can be raised to 60 °C (Fig. 4 B). Five 20-minute heating and cooling cycles were carried out continuously during temperature detection. No significant photothermal performance degradation was observed until the end of the cycle (Fig. 4 E and S4D), indicating that PCD had excellent photothermal stability under 1064 nm and 808 nm laser irradiation. To further prove the photothermal treatment ability of PCD on tumor cells in vitro and in vivo, the photothermal performance of PCD was first tested on MCF-7 cells. It was found that when PCD concentration reached 200 μg/mL, the temperature of the MCF-7 cell-cultured wells in the 96-well plate rose to 44.2 °C under 1064 nm laser irradiation (Fig. 4 F). In addition, in the tumor-bearing mice, after being injected with PCD by tail vein for eight hours, the temperature of tumor tissue could reach 45 °C under 1064 nm laser irradiation for five minutes (Fig. 4 F). Temperatures of 43–45 °C can effectively kill tumor cells and reduce damage to normal tissues during tumor photothermal therapy.[29,30] When treated with different concentrations of PCD for 24 h, the cell viability of MCF-7 cells without irradiation and with irradiation (1064 nm, 1.0 W/cm2, five minutes) show that PCD demonstrated good biocompatibility and had the PTT ability to kill tumors in the NIR-II window (Fig. 4 G-H). Meanwhile, when irradiated at 808 nm, MCF-7 cell-cultured wells and tumor tissue can also reach an effective lethal tumor temperature, killing tumor cells ( Fig. S4
E-F in Supporting Information).

The penetration of PCD in tumor tissues was investigated in vitro and in vivo. First, a large-volume tumor cell scaffold was established to evaluate the penetration and permeation of PCD during NIR-II irradiation on antitumor therapy in vitro (Fig. 5
A). Cell viability under 1064 nm irradiation decreased significantly compared with 808 nm irradiation (Fig. 5 B). Then, MCF-7 tumorspheres were employed to detect the permeability of PCD themselves. After incubating with PCD, the bright fluorescence of PCD increased gradually inside the tumorsphere as time went by (Fig. 5 C). Moreover, after being injected with PCD by paracancerous, the fluorescence of PCD in the tumor tissue ex vivo was gradually increased from the proximal end of injection to the tumor center and then to the dismal tumor tissue (Fig. 5 D). It was concluded that the ultra-small PCD could deeply penetrate tumor tissue, thus providing a basis for photothermal therapy under NIR-II irradiation.

The tumor PTT efficiency in vivo PCD was evaluated on MCF-7 cell-bearing Balb/c nude mice. After implanting the MCF-7 cells for nine days, the mice were injected with PCD via tail vein and were irradiated with a 1064 nm laser (Fig. 6
A). During treatment, there was no significant difference in the mice body weight among each mice group (Fig. 6 B), but their tumor volume changed significantly (Fig. 6 C). In PBS, PCD, or PBS + NIR-II groups, there were no significant differences in tumor growth and the final tumor volume (Fig. 6 C and D). However, the tumor growth in the PCD + NIR-II group was significantly inhibited (Fig. 6 C and D).

Moreover, to indicate the efficiency of NIR-II-based PTT and deep penetration of PCD, the tumor site was covered with a 0.5 cm-thick chicken breast. Tumor growth inhibition remained evident during the treatment, with no significant difference compared to the PCD + NIR-II group (Fig. 6 C). From the H&E staining and TUNEL immunohistochemistry of the isolated tumor tissue sections, it can be seen that the tumor apoptosis in PCD + NIR-II and PCD + NIR-II + Chicken groups was the most obvious (Fig. 6 E). The experimental results show that PCD can effectively kill tumor cells in the body and inhibit the growth of tumor tissues under the light and photothermal effects of NIR-II. Further observation of the sections of the main organs found that there was no significant difference in the morphology of the tissue cells in all experimental groups compared with the normal mice regarding the heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney ( Fig. S6 in Supporting Information), thus indicating that PCD offered good organizational security. The developed PCD provided a potential photothermal reagent for tumor therapy in the future.

Promoting drug penetration into the tumor is a crucial problem for tumor therapy, in which particle size is an essential factor. Nanoparticles about 100 nm in diameter are usually used for drug delivery by their enhanced permeation and retention (EPR) effect. Nevertheless, these nanoparticles were found distributed around tumor blood vessels and little penetrated the tumor parenchyma.[31] However, some work reported that when the size was as small as 20 nm, the nanoparticles demonstrated superior penetration behavior into the tumorspheres than the larger nanoparticles (40, 100, and 200 nm).[32] Further, the ultra-small gold nanoparticles (2 and 6 nm) showed remarkable advantages over 15 nm nanoparticles in the tumor uptake and permeability. Moreover, the accumulation behavior in the tumor spheroids exhibited a size-dependent manner.[4] In our work, the synthesized PCD was about 5 nm in size and showed the deep tumor penetration effect in MCF-7 tumorspheres and tumor tissue in mice. Meanwhile, CDs can be metabolized through the liver and kidney post-injected by tail vein and showed good biocompatibility. [22,33] Therefore, when enriched in the tumor site by EPR effects, the ultra-small PCD can infiltrate into the tumor through the tissue gaps and perform their photothermal functions.

PTT as a non-invasive treatment method has been recognized as a prospective and essential method for tumor therapy. Materials with photothermal conversion properties can convert light into heat and kill tumor cells without harming the surrounding healthy tissue.[34-37] CDs are a widely used photothermal agent that has shown promising application in tumor PTT. Cu/CD-crosslinked nanosheets fabricated by mixing Cu2+ and S-doped CDs can exhibit NIR photothermal conversion and an excellent therapeutic effect in vitro and vivo.[38] Other CDs prepared by a hydrothermal method from watermelon juice showed good biocompatibility in the dark condition and good anticancer efficiency under 808 nm laser irradiation, and the tumor volume in mice shrunk obviously within six days.[36] The photothermal conversion efficiency is about 40–80% in these works.[38-42] In our work, PCD showed a relatively high photothermal conversion efficiency of 83.72% under 808 nm irradiation. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 4 E and S4D, the temperature stability over five on/off irradiation cycles of PCD indicated that this photothermal material in an aqueous solution has good photothermal properties.[16,43] Therefore, our PCD performed good biocompatibility and high phototoxicity in vitro and vivo.

Compared with NIR-Ⅰ PTT, the development of NIR-II photothermal CDs has gained the spotlight at present. NIR-II PTT irradiated by a laser in the 1000–1700 nm range has good prospects for application in tumor photothermal therapy because of its lower intensity and weak light scattering compared with NIR-I light. Moreover, a 1064 nm laser can penetrate tissues with a thickness of about 1 cm, thus having an excellent therapeutic effect on non-superficial tissues and tumor tissues with a relatively complete vascular system in vivo.[9] In our work, PCD also showed a high photothermal conversion efficiency of 77.25% under 1064 nm irradiation. Accompanied with PCD accumulating in tumor tissue (Fig. 3 C) and penetrating deeply into tumor tissue with the ultra-small size (Fig. 5 D), the anticancer photothermal efficiency can be significantly improved by NIR-II irradiation. Meanwhile, PCD had abundant functional groups on their surface, which provide a broad biomedical application according to the surface modification.[44] Recently, Liu et al. designed the orange luminescent CDs modified with transferrin and glucose oxidase to realize gliomas targeting therapy.[45] Yang et al. designed yellow fluorescent carbon dot nanoclusters loaded with DOX and surface-modified with the cancer cell membrane, which possessed the ability of homologous targeting of tumor cells and escaped the phagocytosis macrophages.[46] It can be seen that the ultra-small PCD could be prospective in its application for anticancer PTT in the future.

PCD with about 5 nm was synthesized with urea, citric acid, and DMSO by a one-step method. The functional groups on the surface of PCD provide a broad biomedical application according to the surface modification. According to XPS and infrared spectroscopy results, there were a series of negatively charged functional groups on the surface of PCD, such as carboxyl groups, amino groups, et al., which led to the zeta potential at −15.81 mV when PCD was in an aqueous solution. Because PCD was negatively charged, attaching to negatively charged proteins in the blood during vascular transport was not easy, thus ensuring that PCD could reach the tumor site by EPR effect. This PCD not only showed good fluorescence and photoacoustic imaging effects in vitro and vivo, but it also effectively permeated deeply into tumor tissues. After being irradiated by a laser at 1064 nm, PCD produced a sufficient photothermal effect causing tumor ablation. Also, PCD had good biocompatibility and did not damage normal tissues. Therefore, PCD can be used as a safe, fluorescent, and photoacoustic imaging agent for guided NIR-II photothermal tumor therapy, thus providing a new direction for using ultra-small CDs in antitumor therapy in the future.
The primary reagents, cells, animal model, the methods of structural, photoacoustic characterizations, and calculation of the photothermal effect of PCD were listed in the Supporting Information.

Yu Han: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Hongmei Liu: Investigation, Methodology. Miao Fan: Investigation, Methodology. Shutao Gao: Methodology, Visualization. Dehui Fan: Methodology, Formal analysis. Zhenguang Wang: Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Jin Chang: Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Jinchao Zhang: Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Kun Ge: Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Visualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundations of China (No. 31971304, 21807023), Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province (No. B2021202038, B2019201449), the Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei Basic Research Cooperation Project (No. 19JCZDJC64100), the Priority Strategy Project of the Key Laboratory of Medicinal Chemistry and Molecular Diagnosis of the Ministry of Education (ts2020003), the Hebei Province “Three Three Three Talents Program” (A202003001).and 2021 Hebei Province Doctoral Candidate Innovation Funding Project (CXZZBS2021011). In addition, the authors would like to thank Conghua Qi from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the TEM photograph and XPS analysis.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2022.02.083.

ce:section.ce:para:
